practice.solution: Interior + exterior = 180°, split in the ratio 7 : 2 (9 parts total), so each part = 180/9 = 20°. The exterior angle = 2 × 20 = 40°. Number of sides = 360 ÷ 40 = 9.

topics.title: Perimeter & Area: Triangles and Quadrilaterals
keyPoints: Triangle: A = (1/2) × base × height; the height must be perpendicular to the base.
Rectangle: A = lw, P = 2l + 2w. Square: A = s², P = 4s.
Parallelogram: A = base × height — use the perpendicular height, never the slanted side.
Trapezoid: A = (1/2)(b₁ + b₂)h — average the parallel bases, times height.
Rhombus or kite: A = (1/2)d₁d₂ using the diagonals.
For L-shapes, split into rectangles; sliding edges often shows perimeter equals the full rectangle's.

examples.problem: A trapezoid has parallel bases 8 and 14 and height 5. Find its area.
A rectangle's length is increased by 20% and its width is decreased by 20%. What happens to the area?
examples.solution: New area = (1.2l)(0.8w) = 0.96lw. The area DECREASES by 4%. It does not stay the same — the percent changes multiply: 1.2 × 0.8 = 0.96.

traps: Using the slanted side as the height of a parallelogram or triangle.
Forgetting the 1/2 in the triangle and trapezoid formulas.
Thinking +20% then -20% leaves area unchanged — it drops 4%.

practice.problem: A triangle has a base of 10 and a height of 7. What is its area?
practice.solution: A = (1/2)(10)(7) = 35. (70 is the trap for forgetting the 1/2.)

practice.problem: A square has the same area as a rectangle that is 16 by 4. What is the perimeter of the square?
practice.solution: Rectangle area = 64, so the square's side is √64 = 8. Perimeter = 4 × 8 = 32.

practice.problem: A 10 by 8 rectangle has a 3 by 4 rectangle cut out of one corner. What is the perimeter of the resulting L-shaped figure?
practice.solution: Slide the two cut edges outward: the L-shape's perimeter equals the original rectangle's, because the removed edges (3 and 4) are replaced by equal interior edges. P = 2(10 + 8) = 36.

practice.problem: What is the area of a circle with radius 5?
practice.solution: Area = πr² = π(5)² = 25π. (Choice A, 10π, is the circumference 2πr.)

practice.problem: A circle has an area of 36π. What is its circumference?
practice.solution: πr² = 36π gives r² = 36, so r = 6. Circumference = 2πr = 2π(6) = 12π.

practice.problem: An arc of a circle of radius 9 has a central angle of 120°. What is the length of the arc?
practice.solution: Full circumference = 2π(9) = 18π. The arc is 120/360 = 1/3 of the circle: (1/3)(18π) = 6π.

practice.problem: A circle is inscribed in a square whose side length is 10 (the circle touches all four sides). What is the area of the region inside the square but outside the circle?
practice.solution: Square area = 10² = 100. The inscribed circle has diameter 10, so radius 5 and area π(5)² = 25π. Shaded region = 100 − 25π. (Using the diameter 10 as the radius wrongly gives 100 − 100π.)

topics.title: Polygons: Interior & Exterior Angles
keyPoints: Sum of interior angles of an n-gon: (n - 2) × 180°.
Each interior angle of a REGULAR n-gon: (n - 2) × 180° / n.
Exterior angles of any polygon always sum to 360°.
Each exterior angle of a regular n-gon: 360°/n — fastest way to find n.
Interior + exterior angle = 180° at each vertex.
A regular hexagon splits into 6 equilateral triangles; its interior angles are 120°.
Number of diagonals: n(n - 3)/2.

examples.problem: What is the sum of the interior angles of a hexagon?
Each interior angle of a regular polygon measures 150°. How many sides does it have?
examples.solution: Each exterior angle = 180° - 150° = 30°. Since exterior angles total 360°, n = 360°/30° = 12 sides. (Using exterior angles is much faster than solving (n-2)180/n = 150.)

traps: Using n × 180 instead of (n - 2) × 180 for the interior angle sum.
The 'each angle' formulas only work for REGULAR polygons.
Confusing interior and exterior angles — they are supplements, not equals.

practice.problem: What is the sum of the interior angles of a pentagon?
Each exterior angle of a regular polygon measures 40°. How many sides does the polygon have?
practice.solution: Exterior angles sum to 360°, so n = 360°/40° = 9 sides.

practice.problem: Each interior angle of a regular polygon measures 144°. How many diagonals does the polygon have?
practice.solution: Exterior angle = 180° - 144° = 36°, so n = 360°/36° = 10 sides. Diagonals = n(n - 3)/2 = 10 × 7/2 = 35.

practice.problem: A rectangular box has dimensions 2 by 3 by 4. What is its surface area?
practice.solution: Surface area = 2(lw + lh + wh) = 2(2·3 + 2·4 + 3·4) = 2(6 + 8 + 12) = 2(26) = 52. (24 is the volume; 26 forgets the factor of 2.)

practice.problem: A rectangular box measuring 12 by 8 by 6 is completely filled with small cubes of edge 2, with no gaps. How many cubes are used?
practice.solution: Along each edge the cubes fit: 12/2 = 6, 8/2 = 4, 6/2 = 3. Total = 6 × 4 × 3 = 72. (Check: box volume 576 ÷ cube volume 8 = 72.)

practice.problem: A cube has a surface area of 96. What is its volume?
practice.solution: One face has area 96/6 = 16, so the edge is √16 = 4. Volume = 4³ = 64. (16 stops at the face area.)

practice.problem: A cylinder has a volume of 100π. If its radius is doubled and its height is halved, what is the new volume?
practice.solution: V = πr²h. New volume = π(2r)²(h/2) = π · 4r² · h/2 = 2πr²h = 2 · (100π) = 200π. Doubling r multiplies by 4, halving h divides by 2, net factor 2.

topics.title: Circles: Circumference, Area, Arcs & Sectors
keyPoints: Circumference: C = 2πr = πd. Area: A = πr².
The radius is HALF the diameter — always check which one the problem gives.
A central angle equals the arc it cuts off; the whole circle is 360°.
Arc length = (central angle/360) × circumference.
Sector area = (central angle/360) × πr².
An angle inscribed in a semicircle (on the diameter) is 90°.
Shaded regions: usually (outer shape area) - (inner shape area).

examples.problem: A circle has area 49π. What is its circumference?
examples.solution: πr² = 49π gives r = 7. Circumference = 2πr = 14π.

examples.problem: A circle is inscribed in a square with side 8. What is the area of the region inside the square but outside the circle?
examples.solution: The circle's diameter equals the side, so r = 4. Square area = 64; circle area = π(4²) = 16π. Shaded area = 64 - 16π.

traps: Using the diameter as the radius: a circle with d = 10 has area 25π, not 100π.
Mixing up C = 2πr with A = πr².
Sector PERIMETER needs the two radii added to the arc length.

practice.problem: What is the circumference of a circle with diameter 12?
practice.solution: C = πd = 12π. (24π is the trap for treating 12 as the radius.)

practice.problem: A circle has circumference 16π. What is its area?
practice.solution: 2πr = 16π gives r = 8. Area = πr² = 64π.

practice.problem: A sector of a circle with radius 8 has a central angle of 45°. What is the area of the sector?
practice.solution: 45/360 = 1/8 of the circle. Circle area = π(8²) = 64π, so sector area = 64π/8 = 8π.

practice.problem: What is the distance between the points (1, 2) and (4, 6)?
practice.solution: Distance = √[(4−1)² + (6−2)²] = √(3² + 4²) = √(9 + 16) = √25 = 5. (7 adds the legs; 25 forgets the square root.)

practice.problem: One endpoint of a segment is (3, 4), and the midpoint of the segment is (7, 1). What are the coordinates of the other endpoint?
What is the equation of the line that passes through the points (0, −3) and (2, 1)?
practice.solution: Slope = (1 − (−3))/(2 − 0) = 4/2 = 2. The line crosses (0, −3), so the y-intercept is −3. Equation: y = 2x − 3.

practice.problem: The line 3x + 4y = 24 crosses the x-axis and the y-axis. What is the distance between these two intercepts?
practice.solution: x-intercept: set y = 0 → 3x = 24 → x = 8, point (8, 0). y-intercept: set x = 0 → 4y = 24 → y = 6, point (0, 6). Distance = √(8² + 6²) = √(64 + 36) = √100 = 10.

topics.title: Solids: Volume & Surface Area
keyPoints: Prisms and cylinders: V = (base area) × height.
Pyramids and cones: V = (1/3) × (base area) × height — do not forget the 1/3.
Sphere: V = (4/3)πr³, SA = 4πr².
Cube: V = s³, SA = 6s². Box: V = lwh, SA = 2(lw + lh + wh).
Cylinder surface area = 2 circles + rolled rectangle: 2πr² + 2πrh.
Space diagonal of a box: d = √(l² + w² + h²).
'How many small cubes fit' = big volume ÷ small volume (when dimensions divide evenly).
formulas: rectangular prism: V = lwh, SA = 2(lw + lh + wh)

examples.problem: A cylinder has radius 3 and height 5. Find its volume.
How many 2-inch cubes are needed to completely fill a box that is 4 in by 6 in by 8 in?
examples.solution: Along each edge: 4/2 = 2, 6/2 = 3, 8/2 = 4 cubes. Total = 2 × 3 × 4 = 24 cubes. (Volume check: 192/8 = 24.)

traps: Forgetting the 1/3 for cones and pyramids.
Doubling a cube's edge multiplies volume by 8 (2³), not by 2.
Confusing surface area (square units) with volume (cubic units).

practice.problem: What is the volume of a rectangular box with dimensions 3 by 4 by 5?
practice.solution: V = lwh = 3 × 4 × 5 = 60. (94 is the surface-area trap: 2(12 + 15 + 20) = 94.)

practice.problem: A cube has volume 27. What is its surface area?
practice.solution: s³ = 27 gives s = 3. SA = 6s² = 6 × 9 = 54.

practice.problem: A cone and a cylinder have the same radius and the same height. If the cylinder's volume is 36π, what is the cone's volume?
practice.solution: A cone is exactly 1/3 of the matching cylinder: 36π/3 = 12π. (108π is the trap for multiplying by 3.)

practice.problem: The point (2, −3) is translated 4 units right and 5 units up. What are the coordinates of its image?
practice.solution: Right adds to x, up adds to y: (2 + 4, −3 + 5) = (6, 2). (Option B moves down instead of up.)

practice.problem: The point (5, −2) is reflected over the y-axis. What are the coordinates of its image?
practice.solution: Reflecting over the y-axis negates the x-coordinate and keeps the y-coordinate: (5, −2) → (−5, −2). (Option A reflects over the x-axis instead.)

practice.problem: Which of the following capital letters has exactly two lines of symmetry?
practice.solution: H has a vertical line of symmetry and a horizontal line of symmetry — exactly two. A and T have only a vertical line (one each), E has only a horizontal line (one), and Z has none (it has point symmetry, not a line).

practice.problem: The point (3, 5) is rotated 90° clockwise about the origin. What are the coordinates of its image?
practice.solution: A 90° clockwise rotation about the origin sends (x, y) → (y, −x). So (3, 5) → (5, −3). (Option A, (−y, x) = (−5, 3), is the 90° counterclockwise result.)

topics.title: Coordinate Geometry: Distance, Midpoint, Slope, Lines
keyPoints: Distance: d = √((x₂-x₁)² + (y₂-y₁)²) — the Pythagorean theorem in disguise.
Midpoint: average the x's and average the y's.
Slope = rise/run = (y₂-y₁)/(x₂-x₁); keep the subtraction order consistent.
y = mx + b: m is the slope, b is the y-intercept.
Parallel lines: equal slopes. Perpendicular lines: slopes multiply to -1 (negative reciprocals).
Horizontal lines have slope 0; vertical lines have undefined slope.
x-intercept: set y = 0. y-intercept: set x = 0.

examples.problem: Find the distance between (1, 2) and (4, 6).
M(3, -1) is the midpoint of segment AB. If A = (-2, 4), find B.
examples.solution: The midpoint is the average, so B's coordinates are double the midpoint minus A: x = 2(3) - (-2) = 8, y = 2(-1) - 4 = -6. B = (8, -6). Check: midpoint of (-2, 4) and (8, -6) is (3, -1). ✓

traps: Forgetting the final square root in distance (answering 25 instead of 5).
Mixing subtraction order in slope: (y₂-y₁)/(x₁-x₂) flips the sign.
Perpendicular slope of 2 is -1/2 — flip AND change sign, not just one.

practice.problem: What is the midpoint of the segment joining (-2, 5) and (6, -1)?
practice.solution: Average each coordinate: x = (-2 + 6)/2 = 2, y = (5 + (-1))/2 = 2. Midpoint = (2, 2).

practice.problem: What is the slope of the line through (2, 3) and (6, 11)?
practice.solution: m = (11 - 3)/(6 - 2) = 8/4 = 2. (1/2 is the trap for putting run over rise.)

practice.problem: A line is perpendicular to y = 2x + 1 and passes through (4, 3). What is its y-intercept?
practice.solution: Perpendicular slope = -1/2. Then y = (-1/2)x + b through (4, 3): 3 = -2 + b, so b = 5.

practice.problem: How many inches are in 4 feet 3 inches?
practice.solution: 4 feet = 4 × 12 = 48 inches. Add the extra 3 inches: 48 + 3 = 51 inches.

practice.problem: A ribbon is 250 centimeters long. How many meters long is the ribbon?
practice.solution: There are 100 centimeters in a meter, so divide: 250 ÷ 100 = 2.5 meters.

practice.problem: How many pints are in 3 quarts?
practice.solution: There are 2 pints in 1 quart, so 3 quarts = 3 × 2 = 6 pints. (1.5 mistakenly divides by 2.)

practice.problem: A car travels at a constant speed of 30 meters per second. How many kilometers does it travel in one hour?
practice.solution: In one hour there are 3600 seconds, so distance = 30 × 3600 = 108,000 meters. Convert to kilometers by dividing by 1000: 108,000 ÷ 1000 = 108 km. (108,000 forgets to convert meters to kilometers.)

topics.title: Transformations & Symmetry
keyPoints: Translation slides, reflection flips, rotation turns — all preserve size and shape (congruent images).
Dilation resizes by a scale factor: image is similar, not congruent (unless factor is 1).
Reflect over x-axis: (x, y) → (x, -y). Reflect over y-axis: (x, y) → (-x, y).
Reflect over the line y = x: (x, y) → (y, x).
Rotations about origin: 180°: (-x, -y); 90° CCW: (-y, x); 90° CW: (y, -x).
Lines of symmetry: square 4, rectangle 2, equilateral triangle 3, regular n-gon has n.
Rotational symmetry: a regular n-gon maps to itself every 360°/n of turn.
formulas: reflect over y = x: (x, y) → (y, x)
rotate 90° CCW about origin: (x, y) → (-y, x)

examples.problem: Point (3, -2) is reflected over the y-axis. What are the new coordinates?
examples.solution: Reflecting over the y-axis changes the sign of x only: (3, -2) → (-3, -2).

examples.problem: Point (2, 5) is rotated 90° clockwise about the origin. Where does it land?
examples.solution: 90° clockwise: (x, y) → (y, -x). So (2, 5) → (5, -2). Sanity check: (1, 0) rotates clockwise to (0, -1), and the rule gives (0, -1). ✓

traps: Reflecting over the x-axis flips the y-coordinate (not the x) — easy to reverse.
Mixing up 90° clockwise and counterclockwise rotation rules.
A non-square rectangle has only 2 lines of symmetry — its diagonals are NOT symmetry lines.

practice.problem: Point (-4, 7) is reflected over the x-axis. What are the coordinates of its image?
practice.solution: Reflection over the x-axis keeps x and flips y: (-4, 7) → (-4, -7).

practice.problem: How many lines of symmetry does a regular pentagon have?
practice.solution: A regular n-sided polygon has exactly n lines of symmetry, so a regular pentagon has 5.

practice.problem: Point (3, -1) is rotated 180° about the origin, and the image is then reflected over the y-axis. What are the final coordinates?
practice.solution: 180° rotation: (3, -1) → (-3, 1). Reflect over y-axis (flip x): (-3, 1) → (3, 1).

practice.problem: The point (5, −2) is reflected over the y-axis. What are the coordinates of its image?
practice.solution: Reflecting over the y-axis negates the x-coordinate and keeps the y-coordinate: (x, y) → (−x, y). So (5, −2) → (−5, −2).

practice.problem: The point (3, 4) is translated 5 units to the left and 2 units up. What are the coordinates of its image?
practice.solution: Left 5 means subtract 5 from x: 3 − 5 = −2. Up 2 means add 2 to y: 4 + 2 = 6. Image is (−2, 6).

practice.problem: How many lines of symmetry does a regular hexagon have?
practice.solution: A regular n-gon has exactly n lines of symmetry. A regular hexagon has 6 sides, so it has 6 lines of symmetry (3 through opposite vertices and 3 through opposite edge midpoints).

practice.problem: Point P(4, −3) is first reflected over the x-axis and then rotated 90° clockwise about the origin. What are the final coordinates of the image?
practice.solution: Reflect over the x-axis: (4, −3) → (4, 3). Rotate 90° clockwise uses (x, y) → (y, −x): (4, 3) → (3, −4).

topics.title: Units & Measurement Conversions
keyPoints: Length: 1 ft = 12 in, 1 yd = 3 ft = 36 in, 1 mile = 5280 ft.
Weight: 1 lb = 16 oz, 1 ton = 2000 lb.
Capacity: 1 gal = 4 qt = 8 pt = 16 cups; 1 cup = 8 fl oz.
Metric prefixes: kilo- = 1000, centi- = 1/100, milli- = 1/1000; so 1 kg = 1000 g.
Time: 1 hr = 60 min, 1 min = 60 s.
AREA units square the factor: 1 ft² = 144 in², 1 yd² = 9 ft².
Convert everything to one unit BEFORE computing.

examples.problem: How many inches are in 2.5 yards?
examples.solution: 1 yd = 36 in, so 2.5 × 36 = 90 inches.

examples.problem: A floor measures 12 ft by 9 ft. How many square yards of carpet are needed to cover it?
examples.solution: Convert first: 12 ft = 4 yd and 9 ft = 3 yd, so area = 4 × 3 = 12 yd². (Trap route: 108 ft² ÷ 3 = 36 is WRONG; 1 yd² = 9 ft², so 108 ÷ 9 = 12.)

traps: Dividing ft² by 3 instead of 9 to get yd² — area conversions use the factor squared.
Multiplying when converting to a BIGGER unit (you should divide).
Mixing units mid-problem, like adding feet to inches without converting.

practice.problem: How many ounces are in 3.5 pounds?
practice.solution: 1 lb = 16 oz, so 3.5 × 16 = 56 ounces.

practice.problem: How many cups are in 2 gallons?
practice.solution: 1 gal = 4 qt = 8 pt = 16 cups, so 2 gal = 32 cups.

practice.problem: A rectangular garden is 2 yards by 5 yards. What is its area in square feet?
practice.solution: Convert first: 2 yd = 6 ft and 5 yd = 15 ft. Area = 6 × 15 = 90 ft². (30 is the trap for 10 yd² × 3; 1 yd² = 9 ft².)

practice.problem: How many minutes are in 3.5 hours?
practice.solution: Each hour has 60 minutes, so 3.5 × 60 = 210 minutes.

practice.problem: A package weighs 2.5 kilograms. If 1 kilogram is about 2.2 pounds, approximately how many pounds does the package weigh?
practice.solution: Multiply kilograms by 2.2 to get pounds: 2.5 × 2.2 = 5.5 pounds. (Adding 2.5 + 2.2 = 4.7 is the trap.)

practice.problem: A garden hose delivers 3 gallons of water per minute. How many quarts does it deliver in 5 minutes? (1 gallon = 4 quarts)
practice.solution: In 5 minutes it delivers 3 × 5 = 15 gallons. Convert to quarts: 15 × 4 = 60 quarts.

practice.problem: A rectangular room measures 12 feet by 15 feet. Carpet costs $3 per square yard. What is the total cost to carpet the room? (1 yard = 3 feet, so 1 square yard = 9 square feet)
practice.solution: Area = 12 × 15 = 180 square feet. Convert to square yards: 180 ÷ 9 = 20 square yards. Cost = 20 × $3 = $60.

topics.title: Mean (Average) & Missing Values
keyPoints: Mean = sum of values ÷ number of values.
Always work with totals: sum = mean × count.
Missing value = required total − sum of known values.
Adding a value equal to the mean leaves the mean unchanged.
If every value increases by k, the mean increases by k.
For evenly spaced numbers, mean = median (middle value if the count is odd; average of the two middle values if even).
formulas: missing value: missing = (mean × count) − sum of known values

examples.problem: The average of 5 numbers is 12. Four of them are 10, 14, 9, and 15. What is the fifth number?
examples.solution: Required total = 12 × 5 = 60. Known sum = 10 + 14 + 9 + 15 = 48. Fifth number = 60 − 48 = 12.

examples.problem: After 4 tests, Maria's average is 84. What must she score on the 5th test to raise her average to 87?
examples.solution: Total needed for average 87 over 5 tests: 87 × 5 = 435. Current total: 84 × 4 = 336. Required score = 435 − 336 = 99.

traps: Averaging the averages instead of working with totals.
Dividing by the old count after a value is added or removed.
Forgetting a new value changes both the sum and the count.

practice.problem: The mean of 6 numbers is 15. If the number 27 is removed, what is the mean of the remaining 5 numbers?
The average of x, x + 2, and x + 7 is 12. What is x?
practice.solution: Sum = 3x + 9. Since mean is 12, sum = 36. So 3x + 9 = 36, 3x = 27, x = 9.

practice.problem: The average of 7 numbers is 20. When two of the numbers are removed, the average of the remaining 5 is 18. What is the average of the two removed numbers?
practice.solution: Original total = 7 × 20 = 140. Remaining total = 5 × 18 = 90. Removed sum = 140 − 90 = 50. Average of the two = 50 ÷ 2 = 25.

practice.problem: The average of four numbers is 25. Three of the numbers are 20, 22, and 30. What is the fourth number?
practice.solution: The sum of the four numbers is 4 × 25 = 100. The three known numbers sum to 20 + 22 + 30 = 72. The fourth number is 100 − 72 = 28.

practice.problem: Maria's scores on three tests are 82, 88, and 91. What score must she earn on a fourth test to have an average of exactly 85 on all four tests?
practice.solution: She needs a total of 4 × 85 = 340. Her current total is 82 + 88 + 91 = 261. The needed score is 340 − 261 = 79.

practice.problem: The average of five numbers is 30. If every number is increased by 4, what is the average of the five new numbers?
practice.solution: Adding 4 to each of the five numbers adds 4 to the average as well. New average = 30 + 4 = 34.

practice.problem: A student's average on 5 tests is 84. When her highest score is removed, the average of the remaining 4 tests is 81. What was her highest score?
practice.solution: Total of all 5 tests = 5 × 84 = 420. Total of the remaining 4 = 4 × 81 = 324. The highest score = 420 − 324 = 96.

topics.title: Weighted Average & Combining Groups
keyPoints: Weighted average: multiply each value by its weight, then divide by total weight.
Never average two group means unless the groups are equal in size.
Combined mean = total of all values ÷ total count.
The combined mean sits closer to the larger group's mean.

examples.problem: In a class, 10 boys average 80 on a test and 20 girls average 86. What is the class average?
examples.solution: Total points = 10 × 80 + 20 × 86 = 800 + 1720 = 2520. Total students = 30. Average = 2520 ÷ 30 = 84. Note it is closer to 86 because more girls.

examples.problem: A teacher counts the final exam twice as much as each of 3 quizzes. Ann scored 80, 85, 90 on quizzes and 95 on the final. What is her weighted average?
examples.solution: Weights: each quiz 1, final 2. Weighted sum = 80 + 85 + 90 + 2(95) = 255 + 190 = 445. Total weight = 1 + 1 + 1 + 2 = 5. Average = 445 ÷ 5 = 89.

traps: Taking the plain average of two group means (e.g., (80 + 86) ÷ 2 = 83) — always a trap choice.
Forgetting to put the total weight, not the number of groups, in the denominator.

practice.problem: 12 students average 75 on a quiz and 18 students average 85. What is the average for all 30 students?
practice.solution: Total = 12 × 75 + 18 × 85 = 900 + 1530 = 2430. Average = 2430 ÷ 30 = 81. (80 is the unweighted trap.)

practice.problem: A store sells 3 shirts at $8 each and 5 shirts at $12 each. What is the average price per shirt sold?
The average of a group of n numbers is 10. When the number 34 is included, the average becomes 12. What is n?
In a class, 20 boys have an average score of 78 and 10 girls have an average score of 84. What is the average score for the whole class of 30 students?
practice.solution: Total for boys = 20 × 78 = 1560. Total for girls = 10 × 84 = 840. Overall total = 2400. Average = 2400 ÷ 30 = 80. (Simply averaging 78 and 84 to get 81 ignores the group sizes.)

practice.problem: A 10-pound mixture is made from 4 pounds of peanuts costing $3 per pound and 6 pounds of cashews costing $8 per pound. What is the cost per pound of the mixture?
On a test, 30% of the students scored 90 and the remaining 70% scored 80. What was the average score for the whole group?
practice.solution: Weight each score by its fraction: 0.30 × 90 + 0.70 × 80 = 27 + 56 = 83. (The simple average 85 ignores the different percentages.)

practice.problem: In a course, the final grade counts 40% homework and 60% exams. A student has a homework average of 95. What exam average does she need for an overall grade of 89?

topics.title: Median, Mode & Range
keyPoints: Median: middle value after sorting; with an even count, average the two middle values.
Mode: the most frequent value; a set can have no mode or several.
Range = largest value − smallest value; it is one number.
Always sort the data before finding the median.
An extreme value (outlier) changes the mean and range but often not the median.
formulas: median position (n odd): the (n+1)/2-th value after sorting
median (n even): average of the two middle values

examples.problem: What is the median of 12, 5, 9, 20, 7?
examples.solution: Sort first: 5, 7, 9, 12, 20. Five values, so the median is the 3rd: 9.

examples.problem: Five positive integers have a mean of 10 and a median of 12. What is the greatest possible value of the largest integer?
examples.solution: Sum = 5 × 10 = 50. Sorted: a ≤ b ≤ 12 ≤ d ≤ e. To maximize e, minimize the others: a = b = 1, and d must be at least the median, so d = 12. Then e = 50 − 1 − 1 − 12 − 12 = 24.

traps: Taking the middle of the unsorted list.
With an even count, picking one middle number instead of averaging both.
Reporting the two endpoints instead of their difference for range.

practice.problem: What is the median of 3, 8, 8, 11, 14, 20?
practice.solution: Six values: the middle two are 8 and 11. Median = (8 + 11) ÷ 2 = 9.5.

practice.problem: For the set {2, 5, 5, 7, 8, 8, 8, 10}, what is the mode minus the median?
practice.solution: Mode = 8 (appears 3 times). Eight values: middle two are 7 and 8, so median = 7.5. Mode − median = 8 − 7.5 = 0.5.

practice.problem: Seven consecutive even integers have a median of 20. What is their range?
practice.solution: The median is the 4th number: 20. The list is 14, 16, 18, 20, 22, 24, 26. Range = 26 − 14 = 12.

practice.problem: A standard number cube (faces 1–6) is rolled once and a coin is flipped once. How many different outcomes of the form (die value, coin side) are possible?
practice.solution: Multiply the choices for each independent step: 6 die results × 2 coin results = 12 outcomes.

practice.problem: How many different 3-digit numbers can be formed using only the digits 1, 2, 3, 4, and 5 if digits may be repeated?
practice.solution: Each of the 3 positions can be any of the 5 digits: 5 × 5 × 5 = 125.

practice.problem: A 4-digit PIN is formed from the digits 0–9, and no digit may be used more than once. How many such PINs are possible?
practice.solution: First digit: 10 choices; then 9, 8, 7 as digits are used up. 10 × 9 × 8 × 7 = 5040.

practice.problem: How many even 3-digit numbers greater than 500 have three different digits?
practice.solution: Hundreds digit is 5–9. Case odd hundreds {5,7,9} (3 ways): units even {0,2,4,6,8} = 5 ways (all differ from an odd digit); tens = any of remaining 8 digits → 3×5×8 = 120. Case even hundreds {6,8} (2 ways): units even but not equal to hundreds = 4 ways; tens = 8 → 2×4×8 = 64. Total 120 + 64 = 184.

topics.title: Bar, Line & Circle Graphs
keyPoints: Read axis labels, units, and scale increments before answering.
A circle graph sector is a fraction of the whole: value = percent × total.
Whole circle = 100% = 360°, so 1% = 3.6°.
Line graphs show change over time; the steepest segment is the fastest change.
Comparing two graphs requires comparing their totals, not just their percents.

examples.problem: A circle graph of a $200 budget shows Rent 40%, Food 25%, Other 35%. How much more is spent on rent than on food?
examples.solution: Difference in percent = 40% − 25% = 15%. Amount = 0.15 × 200 = $30.

examples.problem: In a circle graph of a 600-student survey, the Sports sector has a central angle of 54°. How many students chose sports?
examples.solution: Fraction of circle = 54 ÷ 360 = 3/20. Students = (3/20) × 600 = 90.

traps: Assuming the vertical axis starts at 0 when it does not.
Comparing sector sizes across two circle graphs with different totals.
Reporting the percent when the question asks for the actual amount.

practice.problem: A bar graph shows books read: January 12, February 8, March 15, April 5. What is the average number of books per month?
A circle graph shows the votes of 480 people. Candidate A's sector has a central angle of 45°. How many people voted for A?
practice.solution: 45 ÷ 360 = 1/8 of the circle. Votes = 480 ÷ 8 = 60.

practice.problem: A line graph shows a plant's height: week 1: 3 cm, week 2: 5 cm, week 3: 9 cm, week 4: 11 cm. By what percent did the height increase from week 2 to week 3?
practice.solution: Increase = 9 − 5 = 4. Percent change = 4 ÷ 5 = 0.8 = 80%. (Dividing by the new value 9 gives the trap 44%.)

practice.problem: A pizza shop offers 7 different toppings. How many ways can a customer choose exactly 3 different toppings for one pizza?
practice.solution: Order does not matter, so use combinations: C(7,3) = (7×6×5)/(3×2×1) = 210/6 = 35.

practice.problem: From 8 members of a club, a president, a vice-president, and a secretary are chosen, and no member may hold more than one office. In how many ways can this be done?
practice.solution: Order matters (distinct offices): 8 choices for president, 7 for vice-president, 6 for secretary. 8 × 7 × 6 = 336.

practice.problem: In how many distinct ways can all the letters of the word LEVEL be arranged?
practice.solution: LEVEL has 5 letters with L repeated twice and E repeated twice. Distinct arrangements = 5!/(2!·2!) = 120/4 = 30.

practice.problem: A team of 4 is to be selected from 5 boys and 4 girls, and the team must contain exactly 2 boys and 2 girls. How many different teams are possible?
practice.solution: Choose the boys and girls independently: C(5,2) × C(4,2) = 10 × 6 = 60.

topics.title: Histograms, Stem-and-Leaf Plots & Frequency Tables
keyPoints: Histogram bars count values in each interval; exact individual values are unknown.
Stem-and-leaf: stem = leading digit(s), leaf = last digit; 4|7 means 47.
Always check the key — a leaf can stand for a decimal.
The number of data values = the number of leaves, not the number of rows.
For a frequency table, mean = sum of (value × frequency) ÷ total frequency.
Find the median by counting entries in order through the plot or table.
formulas: mean from frequency table: mean = Σ(value × frequency) ÷ Σ(frequency)

examples.problem: Stem-and-leaf (key 2|3 = 23): 1| 2 5 8; 2| 0 3 3 7; 3| 1 4. What is the median?
examples.solution: Data in order: 12, 15, 18, 20, 23, 23, 27, 31, 34 — nine values. The median is the 5th value: 23.

examples.problem: A soccer team's goals per game: 0 goals in 4 games, 1 goal in 7 games, 2 goals in 6 games, 3 goals in 3 games. What is the mean number of goals per game?

traps: Reading leaves as separate numbers instead of attaching them to their stems.
Averaging the frequencies themselves instead of value × frequency.
Using the number of rows or bars as n when finding the median.

practice.problem: Stem-and-leaf (key 2|4 = 24): 1| 3 6; 2| 2 5 5 9; 3| 0 7. What is the mode?
practice.solution: The data are 13, 16, 22, 25, 25, 29, 30, 37. Only 25 appears twice, so the mode is 25.

practice.problem: Using the same plot (data 13, 16, 22, 25, 25, 29, 30, 37), what is the range?
practice.solution: Range = largest − smallest = 37 − 13 = 24.

practice.problem: A frequency table of quiz scores: 6 (2 students), 7 (5 students), 8 (6 students), 9 (4 students), 10 (3 students). What is the median score?
practice.solution: Total = 20 students, so the median is the average of the 10th and 11th scores. Cumulative counts: 2, then 7, then 13. Positions 8 through 13 are all 8s, so the 10th and 11th scores are both 8. Median = 8.

practice.problem: In a class of 30 students, 18 play soccer, 15 play basketball, and 5 play neither sport. How many students play both sports?
practice.solution: Students playing at least one sport = 30 − 5 = 25. By inclusion-exclusion, both = 18 + 15 − 25 = 8.

practice.problem: Of 60 students, 35 take art, 28 take music, and 12 take both. How many students take art but not music?
practice.solution: Art but not music = (total art) − (both) = 35 − 12 = 23.

practice.problem: In a group of 80 people, 45 own a car and 38 own a bike, while 12 own neither. How many people own exactly one of the two?
practice.solution: At least one = 80 − 12 = 68. Own both = 45 + 38 − 68 = 15. Exactly one = 68 − 15 = 53.

practice.problem: In a class, every student studies Latin, Greek, or both. Exactly 3/4 of the students study Latin and exactly 1/2 study Greek. If 15 students study both languages, how many students are in the class?
practice.solution: Let n = total. Since everyone studies at least one, n = Latin + Greek − both = (3/4)n + (1/2)n − 15 = (5/4)n − 15. So n − (5/4)n = −15, giving −n/4 = −15, so n = 60. (Check: Latin 45, Greek 30, both 15, union = 45+30−15 = 60.)

topics.title: Probability of a Single Event
keyPoints: P(event) = favorable outcomes ÷ total outcomes, when outcomes are equally likely.
Probability is always between 0 and 1: 0 = impossible, 1 = certain.
The probabilities of all possible outcomes add up to 1.
Probability can be written as a fraction, decimal, or percent.
Expected count = probability × number of trials.
formulas: sum of all outcomes: P(outcome 1) + P(outcome 2) + ... = 1

examples.problem: A bag holds 3 red, 4 blue, and 5 green marbles. One is drawn at random. What is P(blue)?
examples.solution: Total marbles = 3 + 4 + 5 = 12. Favorable = 4. P(blue) = 4/12 = 1/3.

examples.problem: A bag contains only red and blue marbles. The probability of drawing red is 3/8. If there are 15 red marbles, how many blue marbles are there?
examples.solution: Red is 3/8 of the total, so total = 15 ÷ (3/8) = 15 × 8/3 = 40. Blue = 40 − 15 = 25.

traps: Putting favorable over unfavorable (that is odds, not probability).
Forgetting to count every object in the denominator.
Any answer choice greater than 1 can never be a probability.

practice.problem: A number cube with faces 1 through 6 is rolled once. What is the probability of rolling a prime number?
practice.solution: Primes from 1 to 6 are 2, 3, and 5 — three faces. P = 3/6 = 1/2. (1 is not prime.)

practice.problem: A jar has 5 red, 6 blue, and 4 white marbles. One is drawn at random. What is P(red or white)?
practice.solution: Total = 15. Red or white = 5 + 4 = 9. P = 9/15 = 3/5.

practice.problem: A two-digit number from 10 to 99 is chosen at random. What is the probability that it is a multiple of 9?
practice.solution: Two-digit multiples of 9: 18, 27, ..., 99. Count = (99 − 18)/9 + 1 = 10. Total two-digit numbers = 90. P = 10/90 = 1/9.

practice.problem: A pattern of numbers begins 1, 4, 9, 16, 25, ... . What is the 8th term?
practice.solution: Each term is a perfect square: the nth term is n². The 8th term is 8² = 64.

practice.problem: The letters of the block MATH are written over and over: MATHMATHMATH... . What is the 47th letter in the pattern?
practice.solution: The block has length 4, so positions repeat with remainder on division by 4: remainder 1→M, 2→A, 3→T, 0→H. Since 47 = 4×11 + 3, the remainder is 3, so the letter is T.

practice.problem: Five runners finish a race. Dana finishes ahead of Cruz but behind Bell. Amir finishes ahead of Bell. Elle finishes last. Who finishes first?
practice.solution: From the clues: Bell ahead of Dana ahead of Cruz, and Amir ahead of Bell, with Elle last. Chain: Amir, Bell, Dana, Cruz, Elle. Amir is first.

practice.problem: In a sequence, each term after the first two equals the sum of the two preceding terms. The 3rd term is 10 and the 5th term is 26. What is the 1st term?
practice.solution: Let the first two terms be a and b. Then t₃ = a + b = 10, t₄ = b + t₃ = b + 10, and t₅ = t₃ + t₄ = 10 + (b + 10) = b + 20 = 26, so b = 6. Then a = 10 − 6 = 4. (Sequence: 4, 6, 10, 16, 26.)

topics.title: Compound Events: Independent & Dependent
keyPoints: Independent events: P(A and B) = P(A) × P(B).
With replacement keeps events independent; without replacement changes the next draw.
Without replacement: update both the favorable count and the total after each draw.
Mutually exclusive events: P(A or B) = P(A) + P(B).
Overlapping events: P(A or B) = P(A) + P(B) − P(A and B).
formulas: independent 'and': P(A and B) = P(A) × P(B)
mutually exclusive 'or': P(A or B) = P(A) + P(B)
general 'or': P(A or B) = P(A) + P(B) − P(A and B)

examples.problem: A fair coin is flipped and a number cube is rolled. What is P(heads and a 6)?
examples.solution: Independent events, so multiply: P = 1/2 × 1/6 = 1/12.

examples.problem: A bag has 4 red and 6 blue marbles. Two marbles are drawn without replacement. What is P(both red)?
examples.solution: First draw: P(red) = 4/10. After removing one red, 3 red remain out of 9 total. Second draw: 3/9. P = 4/10 × 3/9 = 12/90 = 2/15.

traps: Adding probabilities for 'and' questions, or multiplying for 'or' questions.
Forgetting to shrink both numerator and denominator after a no-replacement draw.
Treating unequal spinner sections or biased outcomes as equally likely.

practice.problem: Two fair coins are flipped. What is the probability that both land heads?
A spinner has 4 equal sections numbered 1 to 4 and is spun twice. What is the probability the first spin is 1 and the second spin is greater than 1?
practice.solution: P(first is 1) = 1/4. P(second > 1) = 3/4. Independent: 1/4 × 3/4 = 3/16.

practice.problem: A drawer has 5 black socks and 3 white socks. Two socks are pulled out at random without replacement. What is the probability both are black?
practice.solution: First: 5/8. Second: 4 black left of 7 socks, so 4/7. P = 5/8 × 4/7 = 20/56 = 5/14. (25/64 is the with-replacement trap.)

practice.problem: A fair six-sided die is rolled twice. What is the probability that both rolls show a number greater than 4?
practice.solution: Numbers greater than 4 on a die: 5 and 6, so P(one roll >4) = 2/6 = 1/3. The two rolls are independent, so multiply: (1/3)(1/3) = 1/9.

practice.problem: A bag contains 4 green and 6 yellow marbles. Two marbles are drawn at random without replacement. What is the probability that the first is green and the second is yellow?
practice.solution: P(first green) = 4/10. After removing a green, 6 yellow remain of 9: P(second yellow) = 6/9. Multiply: (4/10)(6/9) = 24/90 = 4/15.

practice.problem: A spinner has 5 equal sections colored red, red, blue, green, and yellow. It is spun twice. What is the probability that it lands on red both times?
practice.solution: P(red) = 2/5 on each spin. Spins are independent, so P(red then red) = (2/5)(2/5) = 4/25.

practice.problem: A box contains 3 red, 4 blue, and 5 green pens. Three pens are drawn at random without replacement. What is the probability that all three are green?
practice.solution: There are 12 pens total. P(all green) = (5/12)(4/11)(3/10). Numerator 5·4·3 = 60, denominator 12·11·10 = 1320, so 60/1320 = 1/22.

topics.title: Complementary Events & "At Least One"
keyPoints: P(not A) = 1 − P(A); the two always add to 1.
P(at least one) = 1 − P(none).
The complement shortcut replaces long casework with one subtraction.
"Not" questions: count the unwanted outcomes and subtract from the total.
formulas: at least one: P(at least one) = 1 − P(none)

examples.problem: The probability of rain tomorrow is 0.3. What is the probability it does not rain?
A number cube is rolled twice. What is the probability of getting at least one 6?
examples.solution: Use the complement. P(no 6 on a roll) = 5/6. P(no 6 twice) = 5/6 × 5/6 = 25/36. P(at least one 6) = 1 − 25/36 = 11/36.

traps: Computing P(exactly one) when the question asks for P(at least one).
Forgetting the final subtraction from 1 after finding P(none).

practice.problem: A spinner lands on red with probability 2/5. What is the probability it does not land on red?
A fair coin is flipped 3 times. What is the probability of getting at least one tail?
practice.solution: P(no tails) = P(all heads) = (1/2)³ = 1/8. P(at least one tail) = 1 − 1/8 = 7/8.

practice.problem: A bag has 3 red and 5 blue marbles. Two are drawn without replacement. What is the probability that at least one is red?
practice.solution: P(no red) = P(both blue) = 5/8 × 4/7 = 20/56 = 5/14. P(at least one red) = 1 − 5/14 = 9/14.

practice.problem: A number cube with faces 1 through 6 is rolled once. What is the probability that the result is NOT a multiple of 3?
practice.solution: Multiples of 3 are 3 and 6, so P(multiple of 3) = 2/6 = 1/3. The complement is P(not) = 1 - 1/3 = 2/3.

practice.problem: A fair coin is flipped 4 times. What is the probability of getting at least one head?
practice.solution: Use the complement. P(no heads) = P(all tails) = (1/2)^4 = 1/16. So P(at least one head) = 1 - 1/16 = 15/16.

practice.problem: A bag has 2 red and 6 blue marbles. Two marbles are drawn at random without replacement. What is the probability that at least one is red?
practice.solution: Complement: both blue = (6/8)(5/7) = 30/56 = 15/28. So P(at least one red) = 1 - 15/28 = 13/28.

practice.problem: A spinner is divided into 3 equal sections numbered 1, 2, and 3. It is spun three times. What is the probability that at least one spin lands on 3?
practice.solution: P(a spin is not 3) = 2/3. For three independent spins, P(no 3 at all) = (2/3)^3 = 8/27. So P(at least one 3) = 1 - 8/27 = 19/27.

topics.title: The Counting Principle
keyPoints: Multiply the number of choices for each independent decision.
Draw a slot for each decision, fill in the option counts, multiply.
Repeats allowed: same count in every slot; no repeats: counts shrink by one.
Handle the most restricted slot first (e.g., a digit that must be even).
A leading digit of a number can never be 0.
formulas: counting principle: total ways = m × n × p × ...

examples.problem: Leo has 4 shirts, 3 pairs of pants, and 2 hats. How many different outfits of one of each can he make?
examples.solution: Multiply the choices: 4 × 3 × 2 = 24 outfits.

examples.problem: How many 3-digit even numbers have no repeated digits?
examples.solution: Split by units digit. Units = 0: hundreds has 9 choices (1–9), tens has 8 left → 9 × 8 = 72. Units = 2, 4, 6, or 8 (4 ways): hundreds can't be 0 or the units digit → 8 choices; tens → 8 choices; 4 × 8 × 8 = 256. Total = 72 + 256 = 328.

traps: Adding the choices instead of multiplying them.
Ignoring a 'no repeats' condition and reusing an option.
Allowing 0 as the first digit of a number.

practice.problem: A restaurant offers 3 appetizers, 4 main courses, and 2 desserts. How many different three-course meals are possible?
A code consists of one letter followed by three digits. If letters and digits may repeat, how many codes are possible?
How many odd 3-digit numbers are less than 400? (Digits may repeat.)
practice.solution: Hundreds digit: 1, 2, or 3 (3 choices). Tens digit: any of 10. Units digit: 1, 3, 5, 7, or 9 (5 choices). Total = 3 × 10 × 5 = 150.

practice.problem: A girl has 4 shirts, 3 pairs of pants, and 2 pairs of shoes. How many different outfits can she make using one of each?
practice.solution: By the counting principle multiply the choices: 4 × 3 × 2 = 24.

practice.problem: A license plate consists of 2 letters followed by 2 digits, where letters and digits may repeat. How many different plates are possible?
practice.solution: Each letter: 26 choices; each digit: 10 choices. Multiply: 26 × 26 × 10 × 10 = 676 × 100 = 67,600.

practice.problem: How many even 3-digit numbers can be formed if digits may repeat?
practice.solution: Hundreds digit: 1-9 (9 choices, cannot be 0). Tens digit: 0-9 (10 choices). Units digit must be even (0,2,4,6,8 = 5 choices). Multiply: 9 × 10 × 5 = 450.

practice.problem: A 3-digit code is formed using the digits 1 through 6 with no digit repeated. How many such codes are odd (end in an odd digit)?
practice.solution: Fill the units digit first: it must be odd (1, 3, or 5) = 3 choices. Then the hundreds digit: 5 remaining choices. Then the tens digit: 4 remaining choices. Multiply: 3 × 5 × 4 = 60.

topics.title: Permutations & Combinations
keyPoints: Order matters → permutation (lineups, rankings); order doesn't matter → combination (committees, pairs).
n distinct items can be arranged in n! ways.
A combination equals the permutation count divided by r! to remove duplicate orders.
Handshake or game-pairing problems: nC2 = n(n−1) ÷ 2.

examples.problem: Five runners compete. In how many ways can gold, silver, and bronze medals be awarded?
examples.solution: Order matters (each medal is different). Slots: 5 × 4 × 3 = 60 ways.

examples.problem: A committee of 2 teachers and 2 students is chosen from 5 teachers and 4 students. How many committees are possible?
examples.solution: Choose teachers: 5C2 = (5 × 4)/2 = 10. Choose students: 4C2 = (4 × 3)/2 = 6. Multiply the independent choices: 10 × 6 = 60.

traps: Using a permutation when order doesn't matter — forgetting to divide by r!.
Counting each handshake twice (A–B and B–A are the same handshake).
Mixing up lineup problems (nPr) with committee problems (nCr).

practice.problem: In how many different orders can the letters A, B, C, D, E be arranged in a row?
In a chess club of 10 players, every player plays every other player exactly once. How many games are played?
practice.solution: Each game is a pair of players: 10C2 = (10 × 9) ÷ 2 = 45. (90 is the double-counting trap.)

practice.problem: How many different 3-letter arrangements can be made from the letters of SMILE if no letter is repeated?
practice.solution: Order matters: 5 × 4 × 3 = 60. (10 is the combination trap; 125 allows repeats.)

practice.problem: A committee of 3 people is to be chosen from 7 people. How many different committees are possible?
practice.solution: Order does not matter, so use combinations: C(7,3) = (7·6·5)/(3·2·1) = 210/6 = 35.

practice.problem: In how many ways can a president and a vice president be chosen from a club of 8 members?
practice.solution: The two roles are distinct, so order matters (a permutation). Choose president (8 ways) then vice president (7 ways): 8 × 7 = 56.

practice.problem: How many different arrangements can be made using all the letters of the word LEVEL?
practice.solution: LEVEL has 5 letters with L repeated twice and E repeated twice. Arrangements = 5!/(2!·2!) = 120/4 = 30.

practice.problem: A team of 4 is to be selected from 5 boys and 4 girls. How many teams contain exactly 2 boys and 2 girls?
practice.solution: Choose 2 boys from 5: C(5,2) = 10. Choose 2 girls from 4: C(4,2) = 6. Multiply the independent selections: 10 × 6 = 60.

topics.title: Venn Diagrams & Overlapping Sets
keyPoints: Adding two group counts double-counts the overlap — subtract it once.
Total = (group A) + (group B) − (both) + (neither).
Fill a Venn diagram from the center (both) outward.
"Only A" = A − both; "only" and "both" are different regions.
If everyone belongs to at least one group, 'neither' = 0.
formulas: two sets: total = A + B − both + neither
only A: only A = A − both

examples.problem: In a class of 30 students, 18 play soccer, 15 play tennis, and 8 play both. How many play neither sport?
examples.solution: At least one sport: 18 + 15 − 8 = 25. Neither = 30 − 25 = 5.

examples.problem: Of 100 students, 60 take French, 50 take Spanish, and 20 take neither. How many take both?
examples.solution: At least one language: 100 − 20 = 80. Then 60 + 50 − both = 80, so both = 110 − 80 = 30.

traps: Forgetting the 'neither' region when totals don't match.
Adding group sizes without subtracting the overlap.
Answering 'both' when the question asks 'only one'.

practice.problem: Of 25 students, 14 like pizza, 12 like burgers, and 6 like both. How many like at least one of the two?
practice.solution: 14 + 12 − 6 = 20 students like at least one.

practice.problem: Using the same class (25 students, 20 like at least one), how many like neither pizza nor burgers?
practice.solution: Neither = 25 − 20 = 5.

practice.problem: In a group of 40 people, 25 have a dog, 17 have a cat, and everyone has at least one of the two. How many have only a dog?
In a class of 30 students, 18 play soccer, 15 play basketball, and 5 play neither sport. How many students play both soccer and basketball?
practice.solution: Students playing at least one sport = 30 − 5 = 25. By inclusion-exclusion, both = 18 + 15 − 25 = 33 − 25 = 8.

practice.problem: In a survey of 60 people, 35 read fiction, 28 read nonfiction, and 12 read both. How many people read exactly one of the two types?
practice.solution: Only fiction = 35 − 12 = 23. Only nonfiction = 28 − 12 = 16. Exactly one type = 23 + 16 = 39. (Note: 35 + 28 − 12 = 51 counts both readers once and is the total who read at least one, not exactly one.)

practice.problem: Every one of 100 students studies French, Spanish, or both. If 62 study French and 55 study Spanish, how many study only Spanish?
practice.solution: Since everyone studies at least one, both = 62 + 55 − 100 = 17. Only Spanish = 55 − 17 = 38.

practice.problem: In a group of 50 people, 8 like neither tea nor coffee. A total of 30 like coffee (this count includes those who like both). The number who like only tea is twice the number who like both. How many people like both tea and coffee?
practice.solution: At least one drink = 50 − 8 = 42. Let both = b, so only-tea = 2b. Everyone in the 42 is either a coffee-liker (30 of them) or a tea-only person: 30 + 2b = 42, so 2b = 12 and b = 6. Check: tea-only 12, both 6, coffee-only 24, neither 8 → 12+6+24+8 = 50.

topics.title: Logic & Pattern Puzzles
keyPoints: Arithmetic sequence: constant difference d; nth term = first term + (n−1)d.
Geometric-style patterns multiply by the same factor each step.
Repeating patterns: divide the position by the cycle length and use the remainder.
Remainder 0 means the last item of the cycle, not the first.
Ordering puzzles (taller, older, faster): draw a line and place each clue.
"Must be true" needs proof from the clues; "could be true" needs one possibility.
formulas: cycle position: remainder of n ÷ (cycle length)

examples.problem: What is the 20th term of the sequence 2, 5, 8, 11, ...?
examples.solution: Common difference d = 3. 20th term = 2 + (20 − 1) × 3 = 2 + 57 = 59.

examples.problem: What is the units digit of 7⁴⁵?
examples.solution: Units digits of powers of 7 cycle: 7, 9, 3, 1, then repeat (cycle length 4). 45 ÷ 4 = 11 remainder 1, so 7⁴⁵ matches the 1st position of the cycle: units digit 7.

traps: Using n instead of (n − 1) in the nth-term formula — off-by-one errors.
Treating remainder 0 as the first item of the cycle instead of the last.
Picking an answer that could be true when the question demands must be true.

practice.problem: In the sequence 3, 7, 11, 15, ..., what is the 12th term?
Beads are strung in the repeating pattern red, white, white, blue, blue, blue. What color is the 100th bead?
practice.solution: Cycle length = 6. 100 ÷ 6 = 16 remainder 4. The 4th bead in the cycle is blue.

practice.problem: Al is taller than Bo. Cy is shorter than Bo. Dee is taller than Al. Who is the second tallest?
practice.solution: Chaining the clues: Dee > Al > Bo > Cy. The order is fully determined, and Al is second tallest.

practice.problem: A sequence begins 1, 2, 4, 7, 11, 16, ... where each term increases by one more than the previous increase. What is the 8th term?
practice.solution: The added amounts are 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, ... Continuing: 16 + 6 = 22 (7th term), then 22 + 7 = 29 (8th term).

practice.problem: In a race, Ravi finished before Sam but after Tina. Uma finished before Tina, and Sam finished before Wu. Who finished third?
practice.solution: Uma before Tina, Tina before Ravi, Ravi before Sam, Sam before Wu gives the full order Uma, Tina, Ravi, Sam, Wu. The third finisher is Ravi.

practice.problem: If today is Wednesday, what day of the week will it be 100 days from now?
practice.solution: Days repeat every 7. 100 ÷ 7 = 14 remainder 2, so 100 days is the same as 2 days ahead. Wednesday + 2 = Friday.

practice.problem: Beads are strung in a repeating pattern of 2 red, then 3 blue, then 1 green (6 beads per cycle), repeated over and over. Among the first 100 beads, how many are blue?
practice.solution: Each 6-bead cycle has 3 blue. 100 ÷ 6 = 16 full cycles (96 beads) with 4 left over. 16 × 3 = 48 blue so far. The next 4 beads are positions 1–4 of a cycle: red, red, blue, blue = 2 more blue. Total = 48 + 2 = 50.
